title: Plugin authoring guide
description: v1 documentation deliverable per PRD Â§19. Covers: the plugin contract with worked examples, the three reference plugins as readable source, conventions (namespace selection, observation budget management, when to report busy, plugin-owned identifier surfacing), anti-patterns (subclassing the binding, hogging frame callbacks, unbounded fragments, swallowing exceptions), and the Â§20.2 taxonomy (configuration / composition / subclass / reimplementation plugins) for wrapping existing tools. Includes the extension/exploration/config.yaml manifest convention. The guide can be drafted from the contract alone; reference-plugin worked examples are slotted in as those plugins land.
design:
## Approach
v1 documentation deliverable per PRD Â§19: a standalone plugin authoring guide written so a third party can ship a working plugin from the guide alone, with no need to read host source (success criterion Â§23). Structure: (1) the Â§7 plugin contract walkthrough â€” `ExplorationPlugin` interface, `ExplorationTool`, observation fragment shape, `BusyState`, `PluginContext` lifecycle hooks â€” each with a small inline example; (2) the three reference plugins (lenny-cx6.25, .26, .27) linked as canonical readable source, each illustrating a different plugin shape (action contribution, structured state observation, busy-state hook); (3) conventions: namespace selection (`^[a-z][a-z0-9_]*$`, unique per session), observation budget management (default 1KB serialized per plugin, truncate gracefully, return null when nothing relevant), when to report busy (only for plugin-known async work the host can't see â€” frame work and animations are already covered by the host), plugin-owned identifier surfacing (the Â§21 default convention: each plugin's observation fragment includes the IDs it'll accept as inputs to its tools); (4) anti-patterns: subclassing the binding, hogging frame callbacks via `registerFrameCallback`, returning unbounded fragments, swallowing exceptions inside `observe()`/`busyState()`; (5) the Â§20.2 taxonomy with worked guidance for each category â€” configuration, composition, subclass, reimplementation; (6) the `extension/exploration/config.yaml` manifest convention from Â§7.6 (declares namespace, Dart class, constructor requirements; not read by the host in v1 but adopting now keeps packages discoverable when v2 auto-discovery lands); (7) the Â§7.7 versioning posture â€” adding tools, expanding fragments, refining busy-state heuristics are non-breaking changes so plugin authors should release as often as they want. The guide ships as Markdown in the repo with a stable URL that the AGENTS.md template (lenny-cx6.20) and DevTools panel (lenny-cx6.21) can both link to.

## Constraints
The guide can be drafted from lenny-cx6.3 (contract types) ALONE â€” that's the entire dependency, deliberately, per the v0.5 tightening pass. Reference-plugin worked examples slot in as those plugins (lenny-cx6.25/26/27) land; the guide ships incrementally, not gated on all three reference plugins being complete. The guide MUST be readable without access to host internals â€” anything a plugin author needs that's only in host source is a bug in the contract or the guide. References to specific PRD sections should cite Â§-numbers (the PRD is canonical; the guide is the on-ramp).

## Ruled out
Generating the guide automatically from doc-comments on the contract types (e.g. dartdoc) is rejected for v1 â€” the narrative shaping (anti-patterns, taxonomy guidance, identifier convention rationale) is the actual deliverable and doesn't come from API docs. Bundling a `exploration_plugin create` scaffolding generator into v1 is rejected â€” that's deferred to lenny-cx6.32 (P3, deferred 60d). Waiting for all three reference plugins to land before shipping any guide is rejected per the v0.5 tightening pass â€” guide depends only on .3, examples slot in incrementally.

## Non-obvious notes
Per the v0.5 tightening pass: this bead's dependency was deliberately reduced to lenny-cx6.3 alone (not lenny-cx6.25/26/27) so guide drafting can run in parallel with reference plugin implementation. The taxonomy in Â§20.2 (configuration / composition / subclass / reimplementation) is the load-bearing decision tree for plugin authors who want to wrap an existing Flutter package â€” every taxonomy category needs a worked example, with Marionette as the case study for subclass-vs-reimplementation tension (Â§20.1). The Â§7.6 manifest convention (extension/exploration/config.yaml) aligns with Jake MacDonald's Packaged AI Assets proposal for the Dart MCP server â€” a package shipping both extension/mcp/config.yaml and extension/exploration/config.yaml gets coding-time and runtime agent integration in one package; document this alignment, it's an ecosystem coherence story, not just a file format. Custom-widget plugin guidance (per acceptance criterion) addresses sparse-semantics apps on custom design systems where the host's diagnostic (lenny-cx6.10) points users at this guide for the fix. Trajectory plugin metadata format (lenny-cx6.19) and dynamic tool list construction (lenny-cx6.14) should be linked but not duplicated â€” the guide is about authoring, not internals.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Contract reference complete with worked examples
- [ ] Reference plugins linked as canonical readable source (slotted in as they land)
- [ ] Conventions and anti-patterns documented
- [ ] Â§20.2 taxonomy explained with examples for each category
- [ ] config.yaml manifest convention specified
- [ ] Â§7.7 versioning posture documented (plugin updates = non-breaking)
- [ ] A third party can ship a working plugin from the guide alone (no host source needed)
- [ ] Recommends writing a custom-widget plugin for apps with sparse semantics on custom design systems

title: exploration_riverpod reference plugin
description: Reference plugin demonstrating structured state contribution. observe() emits {invalidatable_providers, recent_state_changes}. Tool: riverpod.invalidate_provider(provider_id) to force-refresh state for adversarial/edge-case testing. Constructor takes the app's ProviderContainer. Validates that plugin-owned identifiers (provider IDs) are surfaced through observation. PRD Â§6.4, Â§12.2, Â§21.
design:
## Approach
Reference plugin under namespace `riverpod` that demonstrates structured state contribution. Constructor takes the app's `ProviderContainer` (passed in explicitly at registration time per Â§7.6 â€” no global lookup, no auto-discovery). `observe()` returns `{invalidatable_providers: [...], recent_state_changes: [...]}` where `invalidatable_providers` is a list of provider IDs currently held in the container that are valid arguments to the plugin's `invalidate_provider` tool, and `recent_state_changes` is a short ring of recently-changed providers (cheap diff signal complementing the host's semantics tree diff). Provider IDs follow Riverpod's debugLabel convention with a fallback to the runtime type name; the plugin documents the format and uses it consistently across the fragment and the tool input. Contributes one tool: `riverpod.invalidate_provider(provider_id)` which calls `container.invalidate(provider)` â€” primarily useful for adversarial / edge-case testing where the agent wants to force a refresh. `busyState()` reports not-busy (Riverpod state changes drive widget rebuilds which the host's frame-stability signal already covers). No lifecycle hook contributions beyond `initialize`/`dispose`.

## Constraints
Implements the Â§7 plugin contract from lenny-cx6.3. Default per-plugin observation budget 1KB serialized â€” `invalidatable_providers` list MUST be truncated to fit (most relevant N, with a `truncated: true` marker) rather than emitted unbounded. `observe()` MUST return null when the container has no providers to surface (e.g. very early in startup). Tool input schema validates `provider_id` as a string. The plugin MUST NOT subclass the Flutter binding or install a global Riverpod observer that conflicts with what the app already has wired up â€” instead it attaches a `ProviderObserver` to the supplied container.

## Ruled out
Auto-discovering the `ProviderContainer` from a global is rejected â€” plugins are user-instantiated per Â§7.6, the container is passed in at registration. Reflecting over Riverpod internals to enumerate every provider type ever declared is rejected in favor of "what's actually in the container right now" â€” keeps the fragment scoped to what the agent can actually act on. Trying to capture full state values in the fragment is rejected; only IDs and change signals â€” full state is too large for the budget and the agent can probe specific values via Riverpod's existing public API if needed.

## Non-obvious notes
This is the canonical demonstration of the Â§21 plugin-owned identifier convention: provider IDs are surfaced through `invalidatable_providers` in the observation fragment, and the tool `invalidate_provider` consumes the same ID format. The plugin authoring guide (lenny-cx6.35) cites this plugin as the worked example for "how do I surface my plugin's identifiers so the model knows what to pass to my tools". Depends on lenny-cx6.3 (contract) and lenny-cx6.11 (harness session) per build order. Sibling plugins: lenny-cx6.25 (router, action contribution), lenny-cx6.27 (dio, busy-state). Risk per Â§22: "plugin authors disagree about identifiers" â€” provider IDs vs route names vs request IDs â€” namespacing keeps them distinct. Ordering pitfall: do not register the `ProviderObserver` until `initialize(PluginContext)` is called â€” early registration races the app's container construction.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Plugin registers under namespace 'riverpod'
- [ ] observe() lists invalidatable providers and recent state changes
- [ ] invalidate_provider tool refreshes the targeted provider
- [ ] Provider identifier convention documented and surfaced in the observation fragment

title: exploration_dio reference plugin (validates busy-state mechanism)
description: Reference plugin demonstrating busy-state contribution AND action contribution. observe() emits {in_flight: [...], recent_completed: [...]}. busyState() reports busy with reason='N in-flight requests' and est_ms while requests are pending â€” this is the mechanism that solves 'wait for in-flight network' for stability policies. Tool: dio.cancel_in_flight() to cancel pending requests for adversarial testing. Validates the busy-state hook end-to-end. PRD Â§7.4, Â§18.
design:
## Approach
Reference plugin under namespace `dio` that is the LOAD-BEARING end-to-end validator for the Â§7.4 busy-state mechanism. Constructor takes the app's configured `Dio` instance. The plugin attaches a Dio `Interceptor` that tracks in-flight requests (start/complete/error) in an internal map keyed by request ID. `observe()` returns `{in_flight: [...], recent_completed: [...]}` where each in-flight entry includes request ID, method, URL host+path (query stripped), elapsed ms, and a best-effort estimated remaining ms; `recent_completed` is a short ring of the last few completions with status code and duration. `busyState()` reports `isBusy=true, reason="N in-flight requests", estimatedDuration=max remaining` while any request is pending â€” this is the mechanism that solves "wait for in-flight network" for the action-relative and quiet-frame stability policies (PRD Â§7.4, Â§9). Contributes one tool: `dio.cancel_in_flight()` for adversarial testing â€” calls `CancelToken.cancel()` across all tracked tokens. The end-to-end validation: drive an action that triggers a Dio request, observation capture must correctly delay until the request completes and `busyState()` returns not-busy, then the observation reflects post-network state.

## Constraints
Implements the Â§7 plugin contract from lenny-cx6.3. Default per-plugin observation budget 1KB serialized â€” long URLs and large in-flight counts MUST be truncated. `observe()` MUST return null when there are no in-flight or recently-completed requests AND no useful state to report (steady state). The interceptor must be installed in `initialize(PluginContext)` AFTER the app's interceptors so the plugin observes everything the app sees. The plugin MUST NOT depend on the host's frame stability signal for its own busy reporting â€” `busyState()` is independent and authoritative for network-pending status. URL query strings MUST be stripped from observation to avoid leaking auth tokens or PII into the trajectory.

## Ruled out
Wrapping the http package or Chopper instead of Dio is rejected for v1 â€” Dio is the most common Flutter networking choice and a single reference plugin is enough to prove the busy-state mechanism. Reporting busy based on time-since-last-request (heartbeat) is rejected; only actually-in-flight requests should hold the stability budget. Surfacing full request bodies is rejected (privacy, budget); only metadata. Subclassing the binding to install a global HttpClient interceptor is explicitly anti-pattern per Â§19 â€” the plugin uses Dio's own interceptor API.

## Non-obvious notes
This bead is the only place in v1 where the busy-state contract is exercised end-to-end â€” lenny-cx6.25 (router) and lenny-cx6.26 (riverpod) both report not-busy. Acceptance criterion "action-relative stability correctly delays observation until requests complete" is the success criterion Â§23 explicitly calls out (â‰Ą95% terminate within budget including correct dio waits). The plugin-owned identifier convention applies here too â€” `cancel_in_flight()` could be extended to accept request IDs surfaced in the observation, but v1 keeps it as cancel-all to keep the validator focused on the busy-state mechanism. Plugin authoring guide (lenny-cx6.35) cites this as the canonical busy-state example. Depends on lenny-cx6.3 (contract) and lenny-cx6.11 (harness session) per build order; the action-relative stability policy lives in lenny-cx6.8 and is the integration partner. Risk per Â§22 mitigation: actually try this against a real app with real network calls before declaring the busy-state design done.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Plugin registers under namespace 'dio'
- [ ] observe() reports in-flight and recent-completed requests
- [ ] busyState() returns isBusy=true with reason while requests pending
- [ ] action-relative stability correctly delays observation until requests complete
- [ ] cancel_in_flight tool cancels pending requests

title: exploration_router reference plugin (raw Router/Navigator)
description: Reference plugin demonstrating routing observation + action contribution. Targets Flutter's built-in Router and Navigator primitives directly â€” no third-party routing dependency. observe() emits {current_route_name, stack: [...], arguments}. Tool: router.navigate(route_name, arguments?) for programmatic navigation that bypasses UI hit-testing. Stresses the contract honestly (no clean RouterDelegate API to lean on). PRD Â§18, Â§6.4.
design:
## Approach
Reference plugin under namespace `router` that targets Flutter's built-in `Router` (declarative, with `RouterDelegate` / `RouteInformationParser`) and `Navigator` (imperative) primitives directly â€” no third-party routing framework dependency. Constructor takes the app's root `GlobalKey<NavigatorState>` (and optionally a `RouterDelegate` reference) so the plugin can introspect the current route stack from either world. `observe()` returns a fragment of shape `{current_route_name, stack: [...], arguments}` for both imperative `Navigator.push` stacks and declarative `Router` configurations; mixed apps are observed correctly because the plugin reads from whichever surface is live. Contributes one tool: `router.navigate(route_name, arguments?)` which performs programmatic navigation that bypasses UI hit-testing â€” the model uses this when it already knows where it wants to go and shouldn't have to drive there through taps. `busyState()` reports not-busy (routing transitions are picked up by the host's frame stability signal in lenny-cx6.4). No lifecycle hook contributions beyond `initialize`/`dispose`. The plugin's observation fragment is what the agent reads to learn valid `route_name` values it can pass back into `router.navigate` â€” this is the canonical worked example of the Â§21 plugin-owned identifier convention.

## Constraints
Implements the Â§7 plugin contract from lenny-cx6.3. Default per-plugin observation budget 1KB serialized (truncated with warning if exceeded). `observe()` MUST return null when the app is not currently on a route this plugin can observe (e.g. during initial boot before the router is wired). Tool name MUST be namespaced as `router.navigate`. Tool input schema is JSON-Schema validated by the harness regardless of plugin source.

## Ruled out
v0.5 changelog explicitly REPLACED `go_router` with raw `Router`/`Navigator` as the routing reference plugin. Rationale per PRD Â§18: simpler, exercises the contract more honestly (no clean `RouterDelegate` API to lean on â€” the plugin must work against whatever the user has wired up), avoids picking a winner among third-party routers, no opinionated framework dependency forced on the host. Validates that the contract can express the most general case; framework-specific plugins (`go_router`, `auto_route`, `beamer`) become straightforward to author by following this pattern. Also ruled out: a unified routing abstraction in the host core (per Â§6.4, this is "unsolvable in the core" â€” routing diversity is the founding motivation for the plugin architecture).

## Non-obvious notes
Plugin-owned identifier convention (PRD Â§21): the observation fragment surfaces the route names the agent can pass into `router.navigate` â€” this is the canonical demonstration that plugins surface their own identifiers through their observation fragment. Acts as the worked example referenced from the plugin authoring guide (lenny-cx6.35). Depends on lenny-cx6.3 (plugin contract) and lenny-cx6.11 (harness session API for end-to-end validation) per the build order. Sibling reference plugins lenny-cx6.26 (riverpod, structured state) and lenny-cx6.27 (dio, busy-state) round out the three-plugin coverage that proves the contract works across all three plugin shapes (observation, action contribution, lifecycle hook). Ordering pitfall: this plugin makes no busy-state contribution â€” that mechanism is validated by lenny-cx6.27, not here.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Plugin registers under namespace 'router'
- [ ] observe() returns routing fragment for both imperative Navigator and declarative Router apps
- [ ] router.navigate tool routes correctly when the destination is registered
- [ ] Plugin works against an app that mixes Navigator pushes and Router config
- [ ] Returns null fragment when not on a managed route

title: DevTools: Timeline panel (live + post-session browsing)
description: Scrollable list of turns. Each row: action taken, one-line diff summary, running summary at that point. Click expands into detail view: full observation (semantics, routes, plugin fragments, screenshot if enabled), reasoning trace, action, validation. Updates live during session; browsable after end. PRD Â§6.3, Â§23.
design:
## Approach
Build the timeline sub-panel as a virtualized scrollable list of turn rows backed by a `List<TurnRecord>` that grows during a live session and is loadable from disk for post-session browsing. The data source is a unified `TurnRecord` value type: live records come from `ExplorationSession`'s per-turn event stream (action executed â†’ diff computed â†’ summary updated, all from .18); post-session records come from parsing the JSONL trajectory format produced by .19, read via DTD filesystem APIs (per .21's persistence path). Each row renders three lines: action name + args one-liner, mechanical diff one-liner (truncated structural-delta from .12), and the running summary at that turn. Tapping a row pushes a detail view that renders the full turn record: full observation (semantics tree, route stack, plugin fragments rendered as expandable JSON nodes, screenshot if attached), the model's full reasoning trace, action + args, and validation result from .17. Use `ListView.builder` for virtualization so a 30-min session (~50MB trajectory per PRD Â§23) scrolls without performance degradation. A mode toggle at the top selects between "Live" (current session, append-only) and "Browse" (load JSONL file via DTD file picker).

## Constraints
- Must read the trajectory JSONL format produced by .19 â€” schema must align exactly, including plugin contribution metadata and fragment versions.
- Live updates must not rebuild the full list â€” use keyed ListView items and append-only state mutations.
- Performance budget: 30-min session trajectories (PRD Â§23 success criterion: <50MB, browsable in panel without performance degradation).
- All file I/O via DTD APIs, never `dart:io` (inherited from .21 / PRD Â§22).
- Detail view must lazily render large fields (full semantics tree, screenshot bytes) so the row tap is responsive.

## Ruled out
- A separate live-only vs post-session UI â€” rejected; unifying both modes through one `TurnRecord` shape avoids two parallel render paths.
- Long-form trajectory analytics (charts, aggregate stats, search) â€” explicitly out of v1 per PRD Â§6.3 and Â§18 ("DevTools panel features beyond the three v1 sub-panels").
- Multi-session comparison â€” out of v1.
- Rendering the full observation inline in each row â€” rejected; budget pressure and scroll perf forbid it. Detail view on tap is the v1 pattern.

## Non-obvious notes
- Depends on .19 (trajectory JSONL writer) AND .21 (scaffold) â€” both must land before this can complete the post-session browsing path.
- The diff one-liner per row is `core` + plugin diff summarization from .12; align the truncation rule (e.g., "+3 nodes, route â†’ /checkout") with whatever .12 produces so the row stays single-line.
- Coordinate with .23 on the shared per-turn event type â€” both panels are downstream of the same session event stream, and a single typed stream avoids duplicate plumbing.
- Screenshot rendering must handle the vision-on-by-default case from PRD Â§11.1 â€” base64-decoded image bytes from the trajectory record, lazy-loaded.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Turn rows render with action, diff one-liner, summary
- [ ] Detail view shows full observation, reasoning, action, validation
- [ ] Live updates while session runs without performance degradation on a 30-min session
- [ ] Post-session browsing of an in-panel trajectory works

title: DevTools: Thinking panel (live streaming)
description: Streams the model's reasoning trace into a scrollable pane as it generates. For Qwen3.6, this is the <think>...</think> content emitted before the action. Surfaces the action ultimately selected and the validation result. Must keep up with generation rate on M3 Ultra hardware (no perceptible token-display lag) per PRD Â§23. PRD Â§6.3.
design:
## Approach
Build the thinking sub-panel as a scrollable text view that consumes a `Stream<String>` of reasoning tokens from the active `ExplorationSession`. The provider abstraction from .14 must expose a streaming API (`Stream<ModelChunk>`) end-to-end: the MLX provider from .15 streams `<think>...</think>` content from Qwen3.6 over the OpenAI-compatible HTTP streaming endpoint (Server-Sent Events), and the harness loop in .18 forwards each chunk to subscribers without buffering. The panel widget subscribes to the per-turn token stream, appends to a render buffer, and pushes characters into a `SelectableText` inside a `ListView` (or a `CustomScrollView` for performance on long traces). After the model finishes generating for a turn, the panel renders two trailing lines: the action selected (tool name + args summary) and the validation result from .17 (success or error). Auto-scroll follows the bottom by default; if the user scrolls up manually, auto-scroll pauses and a "jump to live" affordance appears.

## Constraints
- Must use the provider's **streaming** API end-to-end â€” no buffer-then-emit. Per PRD Â§23, the thinking-trace stream must keep up with M3 Ultra generation rate with no perceptible lag.
- Rendering must avoid full-tree rebuilds per token â€” use a controller-driven append pattern (e.g., `ValueListenable<StringBuffer>` or an append-only `TextEditingController`) so token throughput is decoupled from widget rebuild cost.
- Panel inherits .21's web-only constraint (no `dart:io`).
- For HTTP streaming to local MLX from a web app, CORS configuration must allow event-stream responses â€” this is documented in .21 but worth re-validating when wiring.
- Manual scroll-up must reliably pause auto-scroll; auto-scroll re-engages only on explicit user action.

## Ruled out
- Out-of-process streaming over a separate channel â€” rejected; PRD Â§6.3's in-panel run shares the in-memory session state, including the token stream, directly.
- Buffering full reasoning trace per turn before rendering â€” rejected; defeats the v0.5 KEY USER VALUE of real-time visibility into the model's mental model of the app.
- Markdown / rich rendering of reasoning â€” deferred; plain monospace text is sufficient for v1 and avoids per-token reformat cost.

## Non-obvious notes
- This is the v0.5 KEY USER VALUE described in PRD Â§6.3 (changes from v0.4) â€” the differentiator vs CLI. Performance is non-negotiable.
- Validate streaming throughput specifically against MLX-served Qwen3.6-35B-A3B on the M3 Ultra target hardware (PRD Â§23), not just synthetic streams.
- The action+validation suffix bridges this panel to the timeline panel (.24); consider emitting a single shared event type so both panels consume the same stream.
- Mid-turn interrupt (.31) will hook into this stream's cancellation; keep the Stream subscription lifecycle clean.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Reasoning tokens stream into the panel as the model generates
- [ ] No perceptible lag between token production and display on M3 Ultra
- [ ] Selected action and validation result surfaced after generation
- [ ] Auto-scroll with manual override
- [ ] Provider's streaming API is used end-to-end

title: DevTools: Prompt panel
description: Goal text field, Start button, model selection dropdown, session-budget controls (turns / wall-clock), plugin-enablement toggles populated from the connected app's registered plugins. Hitting Start instantiates an ExplorationSession in-panel. Replaces the CLI's argument parsing for interactive use. PRD Â§6.3.
design:
## Approach
Build the prompt sub-panel as a Flutter widget rendered into the Prompt tab placeholder from .21. The layout is a single-column form: a multiline goal `TextField`, a model selection `DropdownButton` (populated from the providers registered in .14/.15/.16), session-budget controls (turn count and wall-clock duration with sensible defaults from PRD Â§10), a `CheckboxListTile` group for plugin enablement, and a `Start` button. Plugin toggles populate from the binding handshake established in .11 â€” when the panel connects to the running app, it queries the registered plugin list (namespaces, descriptions) via the host's VM service extensions and renders a toggle per plugin, with all enabled by default. Hitting Start collects the form state into an `ExplorationSessionConfig`, instantiates `ExplorationSession` in-panel (per .21's in-panel-run model), and disables form inputs while the session runs. A Stop button replaces Start during runtime to allow clean session termination via the loop driver's cancellation hook from .18.

## Constraints
- Plugin list is dynamic â€” must come from the live binding handshake, not a static config file. If the binding reports zero plugins, render an empty plugin section with a hint pointing at the plugin authoring guide (.35).
- Form must be disabled (not just visually grayed) while a session is running to prevent mid-session config changes.
- Stop control must invoke the same cancellation path the .31 mid-turn-interrupt feature will eventually extend â€” design the Stop wiring to be extensible rather than ad-hoc.
- Web-only constraints inherited from .21 (no `dart:io`).
- Model selection list must reflect only providers actually configured/available in the running harness, not a hardcoded superset.

## Ruled out
- Replicating the CLI's full argument-parsing surface in this UI â€” rejected as out of scope per the deliberately minimal v1 scope (PRD Â§6.3: "deliberately does not duplicate everything the CLI does"). Advanced flags stay CLI-only.
- A wizard / multi-step flow â€” rejected; single-page form is faster for the iterative dev usage pattern this targets.
- Persisting form state across DevTools sessions â€” deferred; not a v1 requirement.

## Non-obvious notes
- Depends only on .21 (scaffold); the binding handshake itself is .11's responsibility â€” confirm .11 surfaces `registeredPlugins` before starting this bead.
- This panel is the user-visible entry point referenced in PRD Â§23's "usable prompt-and-watch loop within 30 seconds" success criterion.
- Design the form-state model as a freezed/value-class so the .31 follow-up (mid-turn interrupt + hint injection) can layer on without restructuring.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Goal entry, model selection, budgets, and plugin toggles render
- [ ] Start spins up an in-panel ExplorationSession
- [ ] Plugin toggles reflect registered plugins from the binding handshake
- [ ] Disabled inputs while a session runs; clear stop control

title: DevTools extension scaffold and auto-discovery
description: exploration_devtools as a Flutter web app embedded in DevTools via package:devtools_extensions. Auto-discovery: when the user's app depends on exploration_flutter, DevTools shows an 'Exploration' tab in standalone, VS Code, and Android Studio integrations. Provides theming, VM service connection inheritance, and the empty panel shell. Per PRD Â§22, harness runs IN-PANEL (no extra IPC) and trajectories persist via Dart Tooling Daemon filesystem APIs. PRD Â§6.3, Â§18.
design:
## Approach
Create `exploration_devtools` as a Flutter web app structured per the `package:devtools_extensions` template: `extension/devtools/config.yaml` declares the extension; `lib/main.dart` boots a `DevToolsExtension` widget that owns theming and the inherited VM service connection. Auto-discovery is config-driven â€” declaring the extension in `exploration_flutter`'s `extension/devtools/` directory means standalone DevTools, VS Code DevTools, and Android Studio DevTools all surface the "Exploration" tab automatically when the user's app pubspec transitively depends on `exploration_flutter`. The scaffold ships an empty panel shell with a tabbed layout (Prompt / Thinking / Timeline placeholders) wired to a single in-panel `ExplorationSession` instance from .11; sub-panels (.22/.23/.24) populate the placeholders. Trajectory persistence routes through the Dart Tooling Daemon (DTD) filesystem APIs exposed by the extension framework â€” never `dart:io` â€” for both writes during live sessions and reads for post-session browsing. The session itself runs in-panel (no extra harness process); the panel is the harness host and the renderer.

## Constraints
- DevTools extension is a Flutter **web** app; the entire transitive code path from this package CANNOT depend on `dart:io`. Trajectory I/O must use DTD filesystem APIs per PRD Â§22.
- Pin a minimum `package:devtools_extensions` version and pin minimum DevTools version in `extension/devtools/config.yaml`; this API is still evolving (PRD Â§22).
- CORS may be required for browser-originated requests to local MLX inference; document the required `mlx-vlm`/SGLang/vLLM headers in the package README so users can configure their server.
- Auto-discovery must not require additional dev-dependency configuration in user pubspecs â€” declaring `exploration_flutter` as a dependency is enough.
- VM service connection must be inherited from DevTools, not opened independently.

## Ruled out
- Out-of-process harness with the panel as a thin UI client over IPC â€” rejected per PRD Â§6.3; in-panel run avoids a second IPC layer and shares in-memory `ExplorationSession` state directly.
- Direct disk writes via `dart:io` from the panel â€” rejected; PRD Â§22 mandates DTD APIs.
- Manual user activation of the panel â€” rejected; PRD Â§23 requires auto-discovery within 30s of opening DevTools.

## Non-obvious notes
- This bead is the gating dependency for .22/.23/.24; finishing the scaffold (including DTD persistence path) unblocks all three sub-panels in parallel.
- PRD Â§22 explicitly identifies devtools_extensions API churn as a known risk and accepts a minor follow-up release â€” bake a version-pin strategy in from day one.
- The harness library (.11+) is web-compatible by construction (PRD Â§22 risk mitigation); validate that no `dart:io` leaked into transitive deps before declaring the scaffold done.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Package builds as a DevTools extension
- [ ] Auto-discovered when user's app depends on exploration_flutter
- [ ] Tab appears in standalone DevTools, VS Code, and Android Studio
- [ ] VM service connection inherited from DevTools
- [ ] DTD-based trajectory persistence path implemented and tested in-panel

title: CLI frontend (exploration_cli) with AGENTS.md template
description: exploration_cli command-line tool. Goal as argument or stdin, --vm-uri, --model, --output, --policy, --plugins (comma-separated). Streams progress to stdout, writes trajectory to disk via dart:io. Right interface for CI runs and batch trajectory collection. Includes a minimal AGENTS.md template under templates/ that --help references. PRD Â§6.2 (CLI frontend), Â§18 (v1 deliverable).
design:
## Approach
Add `exploration_cli` as a Dart console app under the cli/ package (created in .1). Use `package:args` for flag parsing: `--vm-uri`, `--model`, `--output`, `--policy`, `--plugins`, plus `--goal` (or read from stdin if absent and stdin is not a TTY). The CLI is a thin shell around `ExplorationSession` from .11: parse flags, resolve per-tier model defaults, instantiate the appropriate `ModelProvider` from .14/.15/.16, attach a stdout progress listener, and run `session.run()` to completion. Trajectory is written to disk via the .19 writer using `dart:io` directly. AGENTS.md template lives at `exploration_cli/templates/AGENTS.md` and is referenced from `--help` output (exact relative path printed) so users can copy and adapt it. Per-tier model defaults are a static map (qwen-mlx â†’ local MLX defaults, claude â†’ Sonnet defaults, openai â†’ GPT-5-class defaults) consistent with PRD Â§16.4.

## Constraints
- CLI is `dart:io`-bound; this is intentional and complements the web-only DevTools frontend (see .21).
- Must not duplicate loop logic â€” every behavior comes from `ExplorationSession`. The CLI only translates flags to config and renders progress.
- Goal accepted as arg OR stdin (mutually exclusive; arg wins, stdin is the CI-piping path).
- Output path defaults to `./trajectories/<timestamp>.jsonl` to keep CI-friendly conventions.
- Exit code: 0 on session completion, non-zero on harness errors per Â§17 (`agent_stuck`, `connection_lost`, etc.).

## Ruled out
- Reimplementing any loop behavior in the CLI layer â€” rejected; both frontends share .18's driver per PRD Â§6.2.
- Auto-launching `flutter run` from the CLI â€” out of scope; users connect to an existing VM URI.
- Rich TUI (curses-style) progress â€” deferred; plain stdout streaming is sufficient for v1 (TTY animations belong in DevTools, not CLI).
- Bundling AGENTS.md content directly in `--help` text â€” file path reference is enough; keeps `--help` short.

## Non-obvious notes
- This bead and .21 split the harness/frontend boundary referenced in PRD Â§6.2; consult that section for the contract both frontends must honor.
- AGENTS.md template should reflect the prompt structure from .13 (running summary + per-turn diff + last N actions) so users understand what the agent sees.
- Depends on .18 (loop driver) and .19 (trajectory writer); both must be feature-complete before this lands.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] CLI parses required and optional flags with helpful --help
- [ ] Goal accepted as arg or via stdin
- [ ] Per-tier model defaults applied
- [ ] AGENTS.md template shipped under templates/
- [ ] End-to-end: 'exploration_cli --goal X --vm-uri Y --model Z' runs against a sample app and writes a trajectory

title: Trajectory writer (JSONL) with plugin contribution metadata
description: JSONL writer for the trajectory log. Header: session metadata (goal, AGENTS.md hash, build identifier, model identifier, harness version, list of active plugins with package versions). Per turn: full observation (core + plugin fragments), stability metadata, proposed action, validation result, executed action, diff (core + plugin diffs), summary update. Final record: outcome (done | budget_exhausted | harness_error) + final summary. Web-compatible â€” DevTools panel writes via DTD filesystem APIs, CLI writes via dart:io. PRD Â§14, Â§22.
design:
## Approach
JSONL trajectory writer with a cross-cutting `TrajectorySink` abstraction so the same writer logic runs in both the CLI frontend (filesystem via `dart:io`) and the DevTools panel (filesystem via Dart Tooling Daemon APIs).

**Architecture (two layers):**
1. **`TrajectoryWriter`** (in `exploration_agent`, web-compatible). Consumes typed `TurnRecord` / `SessionHeader` / `SessionFooter` values from the loop driver (.18), serializes each as one JSON line, hands the bytes to a `TrajectorySink`. Writer flushes after every turn â€” a crashed session's trajectory is always recoverable up to and including the last fully-completed turn.
2. **`TrajectorySink`** (interface, web-compatible). Two impls live outside this package:
   - **`FileTrajectorySink`** in `exploration_cli` (uses `dart:io` `File`/`IOSink`).
   - **`DtdTrajectorySink`** in `exploration_devtools` (uses Dart Tooling Daemon's filesystem service via `package:dtd`).

The harness library only knows about the sink interface â€” it never imports `dart:io` (PRD Â§22).

**Record shapes (PRD Â§14):**
- **Header** (line 1): `{type: "header", goal, agentsMdHash, buildIdentifier, modelIdentifier, harnessVersion, plugins: [{namespace, packageVersion, contractVersion}], config: {budgets, policies}}`. Plugins recorded with **package versions** so a trajectory remains readable months later when a plugin's been upgraded â€” readers can detect schema mismatches and degrade gracefully.
- **Per-turn**: `{type: "turn", index, observation: {core, plugins}, stability, proposedAction, validation: {result, retries}, executedAction, diff: {core, plugins}, summaryUpdate, modelMetadata: {tokensIn, tokensOut, durationMs}}`.
- **Plugin-disable event** (zero or more, between turns): `{type: "plugin_disabled", namespace, reason, turn}` â€” captures auto-disables (.18) so timeline UI (.24) can render them.
- **Footer** (last line): `{type: "footer", outcome, finalSummary, totalTurns, totalDurationMs, harnessError?}`. Always written even on `harness_error` â€” the loop driver (.18) wraps its outermost try/finally around the writer's `close()`.

## Constraints
- **Web-compatible** per PRD Â§22: the `TrajectoryWriter` and `TrajectorySink` interface are in `exploration_agent` and **must not import `dart:io`**. The CI guard from .11 covers this.
- Per-turn flush is non-negotiable â€” partial trajectories must always be readable. JSONL is naturally append-and-flush friendly.
- 30-min session must produce <50MB trajectory (PRD Â§23 success criterion). Screenshots are the bulk; if defaults blow the budget, document compression or down-sampling toggles in the writer config (don't silently drop).
- Header's plugin-version metadata is mandatory for forward compatibility.

## Ruled out
- A single concrete writer with `kIsWeb` branching: keeps `dart:io` out of the import graph entirely (kIsWeb branching still imports the symbol). Sink-interface split is cleaner and gets web-compat for free.
- Streaming format other than JSONL (e.g., proto, msgpack): JSONL is human-greppable, line-tailable for live timeline (.24), and trivial for the CLI viewer. The 50MB target is achievable in JSONL given screenshot-handling.
- Compressed JSONL (`.jsonl.gz`): trades inspectability for size; defer until there's a real size problem.
- Letting plugins write their own segments to the trajectory: plugin contributions are already captured in `observation.plugins.<namespace>` and diff. A plugin-direct write channel would add a new contract surface for marginal value.
- Buffering across turns to batch writes: defeats per-turn-flush guarantee.

## Non-obvious notes
- Depends on .11 (lives in the harness lib). Blocks .18 (loop driver writes here), .20 (CLI provides FileTrajectorySink), .21 (DevTools provides DtdTrajectorySink), .24 (timeline panel reads JSONL â€” also live-tails).
- The web-compat split is **the** architectural decision in this story â€” note the trade-off explicitly so reviewers don't push back on the indirection.
- Plugin contribution metadata in the header lets future tooling (and downstream trajectory consumers) reason about which plugins were active when a turn was captured. Important for the v2 trajectory-replay feature even though we're not building it now.
- Reading trajectories: the CLI viewer (.20) and DevTools timeline panel (.24) both consume this format â€” keep the schema documented in `exploration_agent`'s README so independent readers can be written.
- Cross-references: PRD Â§14 (trajectory persistence), Â§22 (harness-in-web-app risk + DTD filesystem mitigation), Â§23 (30-min/<50MB success criterion).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] JSONL has typed header line and per-turn records per PRD Â§14
- [ ] Plugin contribution metadata recorded so trajectories remain readable later
- [ ] Final record always written, even on harness_error
- [ ] Per-turn flush so a crashed session preserves progress
- [ ] Web-compatible writer abstraction; CLI uses dart:io, DevTools uses DTD

title: Anthropic and OpenAI providers
description: Frontier-API providers for Anthropic (Claude 4.6+ class) and OpenAI (GPT-5 class). Wrap their respective SDKs / HTTP APIs behind ModelProvider. Capability metadata: vision per model, no preserve_thinking equivalent. Apply per-tier defaults: lower temperature, smaller observation budgets, tighter retry budgets vs the Qwen3.6 path. PRD Â§16.4.
design:
## Approach
Two concrete `ModelProvider` implementations behind the .14 interface:

**`AnthropicModelProvider`.** Targets Claude 4.6+ class models. Uses the official Anthropic Dart SDK if web-compatible; otherwise `package:http` directly against the Messages API. Tool-use translation: map the harness's tool list to Anthropic's `tools` array, parse `tool_use` content blocks back into the harness's `ModelDecision` shape. Vision via `image` content blocks (base64-encoded PNG when capability reports vision=true). Streaming for the DevTools thinking panel (.23): Anthropic doesn't expose a `<think>` block by name, so emit `ThinkingDelta` events from the model's text content stream when a `thinking` content block (extended-thinking models) is present, otherwise from the rationale field of the structured output.

**`OpenAiModelProvider`.** Targets GPT-5 class. Uses Responses API (or Chat Completions if Responses isn't available for the chosen model) with structured outputs (strict JSON schema mode) for the dynamic action schema from .14. Vision via `image_url` content parts. Streaming thinking via OpenAI's reasoning summary content where supported.

Both providers share helpers (lifted into the harness lib) for: schema-strict output enforcement, tool definitions translation, vision-payload attachment, and thinking-delta extraction.

**Per-tier defaults** (PRD Â§16.4): `temperature=0.3-0.7` (lower than Qwen path), tighter retry budget (1 schema retry, no extra tool-validation retries beyond .17's), smaller observation budgets (4KB total vs 6KB) since frontier-API tokens are billed and large per-turn payloads add up. `preserveThinking=false` (no equivalent to Qwen's mode); capability surface reports this honestly.

## Constraints
- **Web-compatible** per PRD Â§22: HTTP-only; pick SDKs that don't transitively import `dart:io`. If the Anthropic/OpenAI Dart SDKs do, fall back to `package:http`.
- API keys come from session config; never persisted by the provider. Frontend (CLI: env var; DevTools: per-session input) is responsible for sourcing them.
- Capability metadata must reflect the **specific** model selected (e.g., GPT-5 vision vs older OpenAI text-only): users select model name, capabilities follow.

## Ruled out
- A single `OpenAiCompatibleProvider` covering both Anthropic and OpenAI: their tool-use shapes are different enough (content blocks vs tool_calls, native vision vs image_url) that splitting is cleaner than abstracting.
- Implementing prompt caching specific to each provider in this story: we use it (caching the AGENTS.md + system prompt where supported) but the optimization is small and self-contained, not blocking the loop driver.
- Local routing/fallback between Anthropic and OpenAI inside one provider: the loop driver (.18) selects a single provider per session. Multi-provider fallback is a v2 concern.
- Custom thinking-trace prompting workaround when the model lacks a thinking content block: just leave the stream empty for that provider; the DevTools panel handles empty streams gracefully.

## Non-obvious notes
- Depends only on .14 (interface). Blocks .18 â€” the loop driver depends on at least one concrete provider being available, and these are the providers used as fallback when MLX/Qwen3.6 stalls (PRD Â§16.4).
- Anthropic's extended-thinking models are particularly suited for the "frontier fallback when local stalls" use case; document this in the provider docs.
- Cross-references: PRD Â§16.4 (other model tiers + per-tier defaults), Â§22 (web-compat for both providers).
- Use this skill's claude-api skill conventions if available (prompt caching, model selection) when filling out the Anthropic provider â€” Opus 4.7 is the current default model class; do NOT pin to retired model IDs.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Anthropic provider supports Claude 4.6+ tool-use and vision
- [ ] OpenAI provider supports GPT-5 class tool-use and vision
- [ ] Frontier tier defaults applied (temp/budgets/retries)
- [ ] Both work end-to-end from the harness loop

title: Memory artifacts and prompt construction with dynamic merged tool list
description: Implements the two memory artifacts and assembles the per-turn prompt. Running summary buffer (model-authored, 500-token soft / 1000-token hard cap, rejects oversize updates). Last 3-5 actions buffer with one-line outcomes. Prompt assembler combines AGENTS.md + goal + summary + last N actions + observation + diff. CRUCIALLY: the prompt's tool list is the dynamic merged set (host core + active plugin tools), regenerated each turn so adding/removing plugins changes the action shape. PRD Â§10 step 5, Â§13, Â§16.2.
design:
## Approach
Three small cooperating components inside `exploration_agent`:

1. **`RunningSummary` buffer.** Model-authored. Token-counted (provider supplies a counter; for MLX/Qwen3.6 this is the tokenizer). Soft cap 500 tokens, hard cap 1000. `update(text)` appends/replaces; oversized updates above the hard cap are rejected with a typed error so the loop driver (.18) can surface a model-readable retry. Soft-cap excess is allowed but flagged in trajectory metadata.
2. **`ActionRing` buffer.** Fixed-size (default 5) ring of `ExecutedAction` records, each compressed to one-line outcome (`tap(node:42) â†’ ok`, `dio.cancel_in_flight() â†’ cancelled 2`). Verbatim into prompt per PRD Â§13.
3. **`PromptAssembler`.** Pure function: `(agentsMd, goal, summary, actions, observation, diff, mergedToolList) â†’ PromptPayload`. Output is a structured payload (system + user messages + tool definitions) the model provider (.14) consumes â€” not a string. Tool definitions are schema-typed for constrained output composition downstream.

**Load-bearing v0.5 design point:** the merged tool list passed to the assembler is regenerated **each turn** from the host's currently-active plugin set (PRD Â§10 step 5, Â§13 final paragraph, Â§16.2). If a plugin auto-disables mid-session (.18 logic), the very next prompt's tool list reflects that â€” the model sees a different action shape. The assembler does not cache or memoize the tool list; it asks the session for the current snapshot.

## Constraints
- **Web-compatible** per PRD Â§22: pure Dart.
- Token counting must work in-browser (MLX path uses tokenizer over HTTP for browser; Anthropic/OpenAI providers expose their own counters per .14 capability metadata).
- Prompt assembly is deterministic given inputs; no hidden timing or randomness â€” important for trajectory replay (post-v1) and reproducibility.

## Ruled out
- Caching the merged tool list across turns: directly contradicts PRD Â§16.2 ("composed each turn"). Plugin enable/disable would silently desync.
- Letting the model author the diff (text summary): per PRD Â§11.3 / Â§13 the diff is mechanical and host-authored; the model only authors the running summary.
- Single-string prompt blob: providers want structured messages + typed tools (Â§14, Â§16); building structure now avoids reshaping later.
- Hard-truncating the running summary on overflow: rejection-with-retry is honest about the budget breach and keeps memory faithful to what the model committed.

## Non-obvious notes
- Depends on .11 (session owns active plugin list) and .12 (diff is an input). Blocks .18 (loop driver assembles per turn).
- The "regenerated each turn from active plugins" design is the v0.5 plugin-architecture payoff â€” verify this in tests by enabling/disabling a fake plugin and asserting the next prompt's tool list changes. This is also what .18's auto-disable hangs off of.
- AGENTS.md is read once at session start (passed in via config from frontend) and included verbatim every turn â€” the model relies on it as stable orientation.
- Cross-references: PRD Â§10 step 5, Â§13 (memory artifacts), Â§16.2 (dynamic schema composition), Â§11.4 (observation budget â€” informs how aggressively to compress diff in prompt).
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Running summary enforces soft and hard token budgets
- [ ] Last-N actions buffer trims to a fixed window
- [ ] Prompt assembler produces structured payload with merged tool list
- [ ] AGENTS.md, diff, stability metadata included verbatim
- [ ] Removing a plugin changes the next turn's tool list

title: Runtime error ring buffer with chained handler API
description: Bounded ring buffer (default 50) capturing unhandled exceptions, framework asserts, render errors. Hooks FlutterError.onError and PlatformDispatcher.instance.onError exactly once at host init. Plugins that need their own handlers chain through PluginContext.registerErrorHandler â€” host calls them in registration order, each returns whether it handled the error. Each entry: message, first 5 stack frames, wall-clock offset. Expose with a 'since' cursor for per-turn delta. PRD Â§6.1, Â§7.5, Â§11.
design:
## Approach
Implement a bounded ring buffer (default 50 entries, configurable) inside `ExplorationBinding` that captures runtime errors from the running app. At host init, hook `FlutterError.onError` and `PlatformDispatcher.instance.onError` exactly once and wire each error event through a chain: the host's ring-buffer recorder PLUS any plugin-registered handlers from `PluginContext.registerErrorHandler` (cx6.3), called in registration order. Each handler returns whether it considers the error "handled"; the host respects that signal but still records the error in its own ring buffer regardless (the buffer is observation context, not error suppression). Each entry is `{message, stackFrames[0..5], wallClockOffsetMs}`. Expose `ext.flutter.exploration.core.get_recent_errors?since=<cursor>` returning entries appended after the cursor and the new cursor for the next call.

## Constraints
- Hook each handler EXACTLY once at host init â€” re-hooking on hot reload or repeated `ensureInitialized()` calls is a defect.
- Ring buffer bounded; no unbounded memory growth across long sessions.
- Stack frames capped at first 5 â€” full stacks blow the observation budget and aren't differentially useful for the model.
- The `since` cursor must support per-turn delta retrieval cleanly; cx6.8 calls this each turn to populate `core.errors[]` in the observation.
- kDebugMode gating (PRD Â§6.1).
- Web-compatible wire format (pure JSON).

## Ruled out
- A separate v0.2-style "error-chaining decision bead" / standalone error chaining mechanism: collapsed into `PluginContext.registerErrorHandler` from cx6.3. Plugins that need their own error handling chain through the host's hook (PRD Â§6.1, Â§7.5); they do NOT install their own framework-level error handlers, which would conflict with the host's single-install invariant. This is a v0.5 simplification â€” one chain, one buffer, one VM extension.
- Persisting errors to disk inside the binding: out of scope. Trajectory persistence (cx6.19) handles durable storage; the binding's job is the live ring buffer.
- Capturing errors from isolates other than the main one: out of v1. Document the limitation.

## Non-obvious notes
- The chaining order is registration order from cx6.3's plugin list. The host's own recorder runs first or last (pick one and document) so ring-buffer behavior is deterministic regardless of plugin handler decisions.
- Plugin handlers may throw â€” wrap each call in try/catch consistent with cx6.3's per-method exception isolation; a throwing handler contributes a strike toward the 3-strikes auto-disable rule.
- The "wallClockOffsetMs" is relative to session start so the model can correlate errors with actions across the trajectory.
- This bead is a prerequisite for cx6.8's core fragment (errors block) and is consumed by cx6.18's loop driver (PRD Â§17 "Runtime error in app under test. Captured in observation, doesn't end session.").
- The "since" cursor pattern keeps the host stateless w.r.t. consumer position â€” the harness owns the cursor.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] FlutterError.onError and PlatformDispatcher onError hooked exactly once
- [ ] Plugin-registered handlers chained in registration order
- [ ] Ring buffer bounded (default 50, configurable)
- [ ] Each entry has message + first 5 stack frames + offset
- [ ] getRecentErrors supports a since cursor for delta

title: Screenshot capture (host)
description: Host emits a PNG screenshot of the app surface. Default ON when configured model is vision-capable, default OFF for text-only models â€” model capability detected through the provider abstraction; user-overridable. Returned as base64 in the observation when enabled. PRD Â§11.1.
design:
## Approach
Implement a host VM service extension `ext.flutter.exploration.core.screenshot` that grabs the current rendered surface, encodes it as PNG, and returns it as a base64-encoded string suitable for direct inclusion in the JSON observation. Use `RendererBinding.instance.renderViews` (or `renderView` for single-view apps) to capture via `toImage` and `ImageByteFormat.png`. The default-on flag is determined by the model provider's capability metadata (PRD Â§11.1, Â§16.3): vision-capable model â†’ default ON, text-only model â†’ default OFF. The harness (cx6.11/.18) reads this flag and only invokes the screenshot extension when enabled, so the disabled path adds zero cost in the binding.

## Constraints
- Default ON for vision-capable models (the Qwen3.6-35B-A3B target case and frontier models with vision); default OFF for text-only (PRD Â§11.1, Â§16.3). User-overridable via session config.
- PNG encoding with reasonable compression â€” screenshots will be carried in the observation budget and sent to the model; oversized PNGs blow the budget.
- kDebugMode gating (PRD Â§6.1).
- Capture must work on every Flutter target (mobile, desktop, web) â€” the host is platform-agnostic per PRD Â§3 goals.

## Ruled out
- JPEG encoding: rejected for v1 â€” PNG is lossless and the size delta is rarely the binding constraint at typical screen sizes; revisit only if budget pressure shows up empirically.
- Multi-view tiled screenshots: out of scope for v1; single-view apps are the primary target. Multi-view support deferred.
- Including the screenshot in the observation budget calculation as a blob: screenshots are sent to the model as a separate input modality (the vision channel), not as part of the JSON observation budget â€” they don't compete with the 6KB serialized text budget (PRD Â§11.4).

## Non-obvious notes
- Screenshot capability detection happens through the provider abstraction (cx6.14), not in this bead â€” this bead exposes the extension and a configuration flag; the harness wires the flag based on model metadata.
- For the local Qwen3.6-35B-A3B target, screenshots are first-class input (PRD Â§16.3 "Vision-language model, not text-only ... Screenshots are a first-class input modality"). The semantics tree is still primary action surface; screenshots provide visual disambiguation and fallback context for screens with sparse semantics.
- Web target: `toImage` works in Flutter web but has CORS implications when the harness runs from a web context (PRD Â§22 "Model provider feasibility from web"). Capturing in-app and returning base64 over VM service avoids the browser-CORS issue entirely.
- Disabled path performance: the harness simply doesn't call the extension. No flag-checking inside the binding's hot path; the binding always exposes the extension and the harness decides whether to invoke it.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Screenshot extension returns a current-frame PNG
- [ ] Encoding/decoding round-trips cleanly
- [ ] Default-enabled flag is wired to model capability metadata
- [ ] Disabled path adds zero cost to the observation

title: Flutter Exploration Agent
description: Build a Dart-based agent harness that drives a running Flutter app via a custom WidgetsBinding, organized as a small policy-free host plus a typed plugin contract. Ships v1 with: host binding (exploration_flutter), harness library (exploration_agent), two frontends (CLI and DevTools extension), three reference plugins (Router, Riverpod, Dio), and a plugin authoring guide. Spec: docs/flutter_exploration_agent_prd_v0.5.md.
design:
## Decomposition (PRD v0.5 â†’ v1 stories)

title: Host diagnostic: warn when interactive widgets lack semantics
description: Connect-time host diagnostic only. Scan for interactable-looking widgets (GestureDetector, InkWell, custom buttons) that lack semantics annotations and surface a clear warning. The fix in v0.5 is to write a custom-widget plugin (the host no longer owns isInteractiveWidget knobs); the diagnostic message points the user at the plugin authoring guide. P3 because v0.5 deemphasizes host-side custom-widget handling â€” the plugin path is the canonical answer.
design:
## Approach
Implement a connect-time host diagnostic that walks the widget tree (or piggybacks on the semantics walk from cx6.5) to identify common interactable widgets that lack semantics annotations: bare `GestureDetector` (without enclosing `Semantics` or a button-like ancestor), `InkWell` without semantic labels, `MouseRegion` with click handlers but no semantics, and any user-flagged custom-button class via a configurable list. Surface results as a list of `{widget_type, location, suggested_fix_pointer}` records exposed via `ext.flutter.exploration.core.diagnostics_warnings`, called once on harness connect (cx6.11). The diagnostic message text MUST point the user at the plugin authoring guide (cx6.35) as the canonical fix path: "These widgets won't be visible to the agent. Add semantics annotations, or write a plugin that exposes them â€” see <plugin guide>."

## Constraints
- Connect-time diagnostic ONLY. Not a runtime concern; not a per-turn observation contributor.
- Must NOT crash or block the session if the walk fails â€” degrade to "no diagnostic this run" with a log line.
- P3 priority by design â€” v0.5 explicitly demoted host-side custom-widget handling. The diagnostic is a developer-experience nicety, not a correctness invariant.
- kDebugMode gating (PRD Â§6.1).

## Ruled out
- The v0.2 `isInteractiveWidget` host configuration knob: explicitly OUT in v0.5. Per PRD Â§6.1 the host does not own custom-design-system knobs â€” that's a plugin concern. v0.5 collapses the entire "host knows about your custom widgets" path into "warn the developer, point at the plugin authoring guide, write a plugin." This bead is the demotion: the host's only role is to surface the warning and direct the fix to the plugin layer.
- An auto-fix or auto-annotation feature: rejected â€” modifying the user's widget tree at runtime to inject semantics is invasive and brittle. The fix path is documentation + plugin.
- Per-turn diagnostic re-runs: rejected â€” the widget tree's interactable surface doesn't change meaningfully turn-to-turn at the granularity this diagnostic operates on, and per-turn cost would be wasted.

## Non-obvious notes
- This bead is the v0.5 corrective for a v0.2 design mistake (the host owning custom-widget detection). The diagnostic message wording is load-bearing: it tells the developer the canonical fix is a plugin, which is consistent with the rest of v0.5's posture (PRD Â§6.1, Â§6.4 "It distributes ecosystem work").
- The plugin-guide pointer is to cx6.35's deliverable; until cx6.35 lands, link to a placeholder URL and update post-cx6.35.
- A sample-app fixture demonstrating both a hit (interactive widget without semantics) and a clean run is part of acceptance â€” this also serves as a doctest for the cx6.35 guide's "wrap your custom widgets" example.
- Depends on cx6.5's semantics walk to know which widgets DO have semantics; the diagnostic is the negation of cx6.5's filter.
